basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Kawthar — “Abundant Good” — is the shortest sūra of the Quran: three verses, of unheard-of density. The word al-Kawthar (الكَوْثَر) derives from kathīr (كَثِير), “much”: it is abundance par excellence. Qushayrī lists the received meanings: “the abundant good; a river in Paradise; prophethood and the Book; the lightening of the Law; the multitude of his community; the companions and the followers; a light in his heart; his knowledge of his Lordship.” The last two — the inward light, the knowledge of Lordship — are, in Sufi reading, the heart of the gift. All the rest (the river, the companions, the community) is its outward outpouring. And the enemy of the Prophet who called him abtar, “cut off”, “without posterity” — the sūra turns his word back upon him: it is he who is abtar, cut off from the good of both worlds; the Prophet, in truth, is the receptacle of a river that does not run dry.

versets.trad: Truly We have bestowed abundant good upon you.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī, listing the senses of al-Kawthar, gives two that speak to the inward path: a light in the heart of the Prophet, and his knowledge of Lordship (maʿrifa bi-rubūbiyya). The gift named here is therefore not only a river of Paradise — it is what the river is the image of: the light of direct knowledge, flowing into the servant's heart without running dry. When this light is received, the rest — the Book, the community, the disciples — flows out of it like water from a spring. Innā aʿṭaynāka, “We have given you”, is in the perfect: the gift is already made. There is no more to wait — only to answer.

mots.def: emphasis particle inna + suffix pronoun We: “truly, We”.
aʿṭā: to give, to grant, to bestow; perfect 1st pers. plur. + suffix pronoun 2nd pers. masc. sing.: We have given you.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 1st pers. plur.

mots.def: al-Kawthar: intensive form of the root k-th-r (abundance); abundance itself — a river in Paradise, but also, in Sufi reading, the light of received knowledge in the heart.

versets.trad: So pray to your Lord and offer sacrifice!
versets.commentaire: The response to the gift is not another request: it is the offering of oneself. Ṣalli (صَلِّ), pray. Wa-nḥar (وَانْحَرْ), sacrifice. Literally, naḥr names the slaughter at the hollow of the neck (between the collarbone and the throat). Qushayrī reports a precious variant: according to ʿAlī, when the verse came down, the Prophet asked Gabriel what this sacrifice was. Gabriel answered: “It is not a sacrifice; He commands you, when you enter prayer, to lift your hands to the level of the naḥr [the hollow of the neck] saying Allāhu akbar.” An astonishing reading: the sacrifice is the very gesture by which one begins prayer — raising the hands, as if to hand oneself over. The offering of one's being is worth the blood of an animal. Another tradition adds: “place your right hand upon your left, beneath your neck, in prayer”. The servant himself is the offering.

mots.def: naḥara: to sacrifice by slitting the neck at the hollow; in a reading reported by ʿAlī, “lift the hands to the level of the neck” at the opening of prayer.

versets.trad: Truly your enemy shall be the one without posterity.
versets.commentaire: al-abtar (الأَبْتَر) — literally: the animal whose tail is cut. Figuratively: the one whose posterity is broken off. Tustarī adds the spiritual sense: abtar is the one who is cut off from the good of both worlds. And Qushayrī, more soberly: “the one of whom no good is remembered; from whom every good is severed.” The verse thus turns back upon those who called the Prophet abtar because his sons had died in infancy: the true abtar is the enemy of the received light. The Sufi reading goes further: every man who cuts himself off from the source — who refuses the offered Kawthar — becomes himself abtar, without true posterity, that is, without anything that lasts after him. The sūra, in three verses, says one thing only: receive the gift, answer by the offering of yourself, and do not be cut off.

mots.def: shāniʾ: one who hates, who scorns; active participle of shanaʾa. With the suffix pronoun: your enemy, the one who hates you.
personal pronoun 3rd pers. masc. sing.: he; here as a pronoun of separation.
abtar: literally, with a severed tail; figuratively, one whose posterity is extinguished; in the spiritual sense (Tustarī), cut off from the good of both worlds.
